revision.text:
[[de:Krönung (Pharao)]] '''Coronation (Pharaoh)''' is an ancient Egyptian festival ==Overview== {{Infobox hieroglyphs |TITLE = Coronation |NAME = M23:X1-L2:X1-N35-N28chai-en-nisut-biti
''ḫˁj-n-njswt-bjtj''
''Appearance (Coronation) of the King'' }} The '''Coronation''' of an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egyptian|1=Ancient Egypt}} king ({{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|qid=Q37110|lt=Pharaoh|1=Pharaoh}}) was understood by the Egyptians as the divine confirmation of the previously {{ill|Designation (law)|en|Designation (law)|qid=Q1200681|lt=designated|1=Designation (law)}} successor. In rare cases of missing designation by the predecessor, the {{ill|Ennead|en|Ennead|qid=Q233458|lt=judgment of the divine council|1=Ennead}} was invoked instead. Throughout Egyptian history, the associated {{ill|Ceremony|en|Ceremony|qid=Q2627975|lt=ceremony|1=Ceremony}} underwent few changes and was always linked to a special holiday, {{ill|Egyptian mythology|en|Egyptian mythology|qid=Q205740|lt=mythologically|1=Egyptian mythology}} associated with a "festival of renewal." == Succession and Ascension == Before the coronation, the official elevation of the successor took place during the reigning king’s lifetime. This ritual required that only biological sons could be publicly declared future successors.Siegfried Schott: ''Der Denkstein Sethos’ I. für die Kapelle Ramses’ I. in Abydos.'' Göttingen 1965, p. 45. {{ill|Ramesses II|en|Ramesses II|qid=Q1523|lt=Ramses II|1=Ramesses II}} reported his designation: {{Quote|When my father appeared before the people, I was the child on his lap. He said concerning me: Crown him king, so that I may behold his beauty while I live. He summoned the chamberlains to place the crown upon my forehead: Place the crown-serpent upon his head, that he may maintain the land, lead the army, and rule over humanity.|Designation by {{ill|Seti I|en|Seti I|qid=Q157918|lt=Seti I|1=Seti I}}|qq=}} Following the king's death, the ascension occurred the next morning at sunrise invoking the {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|qid=Q511056|lt=sun god|1=Solar deity}}; initially {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}} as the sky deity until the {{ill|Fourth Dynasty|de|4. Dynastie (Ägypten)|ja|第4王朝 (エジプト)|zh|第四王朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fourth Dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, subsequently {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Ra|1=Ra}} until the {{ill|Second Intermediate Period of Egypt|en|Second Intermediate Period of Egypt|qid=Q206715|lt=Second Intermediate Period|1=Second Intermediate Period of Egypt}}, and finally {{ill|Amun-Ra|de|Amun-Re|1=Amun-Ra|2=de|3=Amun-Re|4=fr|5=Amon-Rê|ja|アメン・ラー|zh|阿蒙-拉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amun-Ra|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}} onwards. Only after the ascension were divine prerequisites met for the coronation. Between the predecessor’s death and coronation, the designated king acted as regent. However, in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|qid=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar|1=Egyptian calendar}}, ascension alone was not recognized as official governance, thus only the symbolic {{ill|unification of Upper and Lower Egypt|de|Vereinigungsfest (Altes Ägypten)|ja|上下エジプト統一祭|zh|上下埃及统一庆典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/unification of Upper and Lower Egypt|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} marked the recurring annual coronation festival. At the end of the seventy-day {{ill|mummification|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|ja|古代エジプトのミイラ作り|zh|古埃及木乃伊制作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummification|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the final phase before coronation began. The designated successor organized the burial festivities for the predecessor, often on the {{ill|second lunar month day|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)|ja|エジプト月暦|zh|埃及阴历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/second lunar month day|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the New Kingdom. Only through the predecessor’s heavenly ascent could the successor secure divine endorsement. == Coronation Ceremony == === Night Ceremony === During the night ceremony, often on the first lunar month day, the designated king was laid upon a bed in the holy of holies. Four wooden seals were placed under his head. At this point, he was already in a state symbolizing death. This was followed by the ritual awakening and symbolic beheading of plants to ward off evil powers. In the {{ill|House of life in Ancient Egypt|en|House of life in Ancient Egypt|qid=Q3279822|lt=House of Life|1=House of life in Ancient Egypt}}, priests presented nine different birds to the king. Wings of a {{ill|Falke|en|Falke|qid=Q240568|lt=falcon|1=Falke}} and then a {{ill|Aegypiinae|en|Aegypiinae|qid=Q13428438|lt=vulture|1=Aegypiinae}} were placed around his cheeks. === Day Ceremony === [[File:Temple of amun karnak.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Karnak (disambiguation)|en|Karnak (disambiguation)|qid=Q403182|lt=Karnak|1=Karnak (disambiguation)}} Temple Complex]] In the morning, the future king proceeded to the ritual palace near the {{ill|Precinct of Amun-Re|en|Precinct of Amun-Re|qid=Q1306397|lt=Temple of Amun-Ra (Karnak)|1=Precinct of Amun-Re}}, awaiting the procession at sunrise. After arriving at the ritual palace's great double gate, the procession moved toward the western gate. The designated king emerged shortly thereafter and prostrated himself before the Amun statue, carried by priests on a litter. After seating the king on the litter before Amun, they proceeded to the {{ill|Maat|en|Maat|qid=Q170471|lt=Temple of Maat|1=Maat}} near the {{ill|Iaru Lake|de|Merencha|ja|イアル湖|zh|伊阿鲁湖|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iaru Lake|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for symbolic cleansing. Returning to the Amun-Ra temple, the ninefold anointing occurred, followed by the ceremonial master granting the king the divine regalia in the holy of holies, including the {{ill|Ankh|en|Ankh|qid=Q180439|lt=ankh|1=Ankh}}, {{ill|Was-sceptre|en|Was-sceptre|qid=Q2341121|lt=was scepter|1=Was-sceptre}}, white leather sandals, and the foreign lands’ staff. After dressing, the king seated himself on the block throne, alternately wearing the white crown of the South, red crown of the North, and the {{ill|Pschent|en|Pschent|qid=Q1141649|lt=Double Crown|1=Pschent}} symbolizing Egypt's unification. The king then wore the ostrich-feathered {{ill|Khepresh|en|Khepresh|qid=Q1070190|lt=Khepresh crown|1=Khepresh}} to the nearby bark sanctuary of the {{ill|divine shadow|de|Gottesschatten (Ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|神の影|zh|神影|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/divine shadow|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to receive his throne name. Further titles followed: {{ill|Horus name|en|Horus name|qid=Q153263|lt=Horus name|1=Horus name}}, {{ill|Nebty name|en|Nebty name|qid=Q15523428|lt=Nebti name|1=Nebty name}}, and {{ill|Golden Horus name|de|Goldname|ja|黄金のホルス名|zh|金荷鲁斯名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Golden Horus name|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 五重称号|1=Ancient Egyptian royal titulary|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q263595}}. Finally, after another anointing, a priest concluded the coronation by giving the king a resin-bread {{ill|Egyptian hieroglyphs|en|Egyptian hieroglyphs|qid=Q132659|lt=hieroglyph|1=Egyptian hieroglyphs}} of power to consume. Sacrificing seven clay animal-shaped gods from the House of Life ended the ritual, allowing the new king to greet the jubilant populace in a golden chariot. ==References== {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar|qq=}} {{translated page|de|Krönung (Pharao)|version=238270950|comment=Imported full de history}} [[ja:戴冠式 (古代エジプト)]] [[de:Krönung (Pharao)]] {{wikidata link|Q1790650}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:Coronations by monarchy]] [[Category:Festivals of ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Kingship (Egypt)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]
